agent1_raw: The model cannot be justified for this deployment. Historical authorization outcomes encode payer policy at a specific point in time, not a stable medical standard. As oncology guidelines evolve, the model will produce lagged outputs. More critically, deploying automated denials in oncology — where a wrongly denied authorization can delay chemotherapy or targeted therapy — carries asymmetric patient harm that agreement metrics do not capture. The evaluation also lacks subgroup performance by cancer type and therapy line, which is essential in a domain this heterogeneous.
agent2_raw: Four issues make this retrospective study insufficient for the proposed deployment. First, training labels reflect historical payer policy decisions, not stable oncology appropriateness — the target shifts as guidelines evolve. Second, urgent cases handled via expedited physician-to-physician review are systematically excluded from the structured training data, creating a dangerous blind spot. Third, aggregate agreement metrics conceal performance variation across cancer types, therapy lines, and policy vintages. Fourth, the deployment conflates auto-approval (lower risk) with auto-denial (higher risk) without demonstrating that the model can make accurate denials under current policy. The business case for faster processing is legitimate, but this evidence does not support the deployment scope proposed.
agent3_raw: The utilization-management committee should decline to authorize this deployment on current evidence. The fundamental issue is that the training labels — historical authorization outcomes — encode the payer's decision-making under policy that changed multiple times during the study period. This is not a stable proxy for future appropriateness. The vendor's aggregate agreement rate tells us the model can reproduce past decisions well, but not whether those decisions align with current guidelines or will align with future ones. Urgent expedited-review cases are also poorly captured. Most importantly, the deployment includes automated denials, and incorrect oncology denials are not correctable on the same timeline as incorrect approvals.
synthesis_raw: All three agents converge on critique_wins. The shared core issues are: (1) training labels encode shifting payer policy, not stable medical truth; (2) urgent cases are underrepresented; (3) aggregate metrics hide subgroup failures; (4) auto-denials carry asymmetric harm. No empirically resolvable disagreement exists among agents — all three conclude the retrospective evidence is structurally insufficient for the proposed deployment scope. ETD: no empirical test needed to establish this conclusion; the structural problems are identifiable from the study design alone.
all_issues_raised: Training labels encode shifting payer policy rather than stable medical appropriateness
Urgent expedited-review cases underrepresented in structured training data
Aggregate agreement metrics hide cancer type and therapy line performance variation
Auto-denials carry asymmetric treatment-delay patient harm
